point_001: The analysis section (Table 5 / Section 4.3.4) is confusing and insufficiently explained: key terms like 'Hits@1', 'Hits@20', and category labels (Not Recit, hits@20-recit., hits@20-path) are not clearly defined, and the logical structure of the categorization is difficult to follow without a diagram or clearer prose.
point_002: The paper does not adequately address the impact of incorrect or low-quality recitations on downstream answer accuracy, nor does it characterize the trade-off between recitation diversity and factual accuracy when varying sampling temperature.
point_003: Computational cost and inference speed of the proposed methods are not reported or compared against baselines (e.g., standard prompting with more shots, BM25 retrieval, or chain-of-thought), making it unclear whether the gains justify the added expense.
point_004: The rationale for choosing the number of few-shot examples for each model and baseline is not justified; different models are tested with inconsistent shot counts (e.g., 16 vs. 64 shots), and the effect of increasing shots on performance is not systematically studied.
point_005: The paper lacks experiments on larger or more capable publicly available language models (e.g., GPT-3, InstructGPT/davinci-002, PaLM, FLAN, T0), making it unclear whether recitation benefits scale with model size or instruction tuning.
point_006: The proposed method still significantly underperforms recent few-shot retrieval-augmented systems (e.g., ATLAS achieves 42% on NQ with a smaller model vs. 32% here), and the paper does not sufficiently acknowledge or discuss this performance gap or the practical advantages of retrieval.
point_007: The passage-hint fine-tuning approach is only evaluated on a subset of datasets/models, requires fine-tuning on Wikipedia data (raising concerns about overfitting to Wikipedia and potential test-set leakage), and its added complexity over vanilla recitation is not well justified.
point_008: The paper does not discuss whether the method handles tail-distribution or out-of-training-distribution knowledge (e.g., post-training events), which is a key advantage of retrieval-augmented systems and an important limitation of purely parametric approaches.
point_009: Figure 2 and the multi-path/multi-hop prompting setup are insufficiently explained: it is unclear whether QA pairs inserted between recitation and question are related to the target question, whether long-distance context issues arise, and how numbered prompts elicit topically distinct recitations in one decoding pass.
point_010: The paper provides little qualitative analysis of generated recitations (e.g., length, resemblance to ground-truth passages, percentage containing correct answers, success and failure cases), which would help readers understand when and why the method works.
point_011: There is an unexplained numerical inconsistency between Table 1 and Table 4 for the same model configuration (LM-Recitation 5-shot: 16.34 vs. 14.16 on the same dataset).
point_012: The motivations given for recitation—matching the causal LM pre-training objective and analogy to human recitation behavior—are not well-supported; the pre-training objective argument is questionable, and no citations are provided for the human cognition claim.
point_013: The paper does not compare the effect of sampling multiple paths for direct prompting or chain-of-thought baselines (i.e., self-consistency applied to those baselines), making it hard to attribute gains specifically to the recitation step rather than to the multi-path aggregation strategy.
